message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objectives</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To assess the degree to which we can replicate a study between a regional and a national database of electronic health record data in the United Kingdom.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>A replication of a retrospective cohort study.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Setting</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Observational EHR data from primary and secondary care sources in the UK. The original study used data from a large, urbanised region (Greater Manchester Care Record, Greater Manchester, UK). This replication study used a national database covering the whole of England, UK (NHS England’s Secure Data Environment service for England, accessed via the BHF Data Science Centre’s CVD-COVID-UK/COVID-IMPACT Consortium).</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Individuals with a diagnosis of T1D or T2D prior to a positive COVID-19 test result. The matched controls (3:1) were individuals who had a positive COVID-19 test result, but who did not have a diagnosis of diabetes on the date of their positive COVID-19 test result. Matching was done on age at COVID-19 diagnosis, sex and approximate date of COVID-19 test.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Primary and secondary outcome measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Hospitalization within 28 days of a positive COVID-19 test.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>We found that many of the effect sizes did not show a statistically significant difference. Where effect sizes were statistically significant in the regional study, then they remained significant in the national study and the effect size was the same direction and of similar magnitude.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>There is some evidence that the findings from studies in smaller regional datasets can be extrapolated to a larger, national setting. However, there were some significant differences and therefore replication studies remain an essential part of healthcare research.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Strengths and limitations of this study</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The same team performed the original study and this replication study</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The underlying data sources, while similar, had differences that may have affected the results</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>The focus of replication was a single outcome for a single condition and may not generalise to other disease areas</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: Replicating a COVID-19 study in a national England database to assess the generalisability of research with regional electronic health record data

reference.unstructured: NHS Digital, General Practice Extraction Service (GPES) Data for pandemic planning and research: a guide for analysts and users of the data, (n.d.). https://digital.nhs.uk/coronavirus/gpes-data-for-pandemic-planning-and-research/guide-for-analysts-and-users-of-the-data (accessed May 31, 2023).
NHS Digital, Hospital Episode Statistics (HES), (n.d.). https://digital.nhs.uk/data-and-information/data-tools-and-services/data-services/hospital-episode-statistics (accessed June 29, 2023).

NHS Digital, COVID-19 Second Generation Surveillance System (SGSS), (n.d.). https://digital.nhs.uk/services/data-access-request-service-dars/dars-products-and-services/data-set-catalogue/covid-19-second-generation-surveillance-system-sgss (accessed June 29, 2023).

reference.article-title: Routinely-collected general practice data are complex, but with systematic processing can be used for quality improvement and research
